yyuJdToZ9xo.title: kaputu bemba teacher lesson 1
yyuJdToZ9xo.prompt_used: This is a Bemba (Icibemba) language lesson by Kaputu. The instructor teaches Bemba vocabulary, grammar, and everyday phrases. Common Bemba: mulishani, natotela, eya, awe, ishina lyandi, ukubomba (to w...

segments.text: Do you wish to learn Bemba or other local languages in Zambia?
Don't worry! Mr. Kaputu also had a native language teacher
based in Zambia. I'm able to teach how to write, speak, and translate.
Subscribe to my channel today and learn how to speak, write, and translate one of the most
languages used in Zambia, Bemba.
Welcome to Bemba lesson 1. Today we are going to look at how
Bemba is spoken or how it is written. We all know that in every language there is an alphabet or
letters to form the words. We are going to start with the letters, the vowels, how they are
pronounced in Bemba. So we have the letter A, we have the letter E, we have the letter I,
we have the letter O, and we have the letter U. These are the vowels we know in English.
But these vowels, the way they are pronounced in Bemba is the A in English is actually A
and the E in English is actually E and the O in English is also O in Bemba. The I in English
is E in Bemba and the U in English is actually O in Bemba. So if we get that correctly and we
remember that, the vowels, the O is the U, the O is the O, the E is the I, the A is the E,
and the R is the A. I hope you got that perfectly. And now we want to give an example how we can
write a word or how we can greet somebody. If you want to greet somebody, you say murishani.
So the word murishani, as we looked at the vowels, we are going to use the letter M
with the letter U and that forms mu. And shani, we are going to use the letter S
and the letter H and then we use I which is E in Bemba and it is sh. The sound is sh.
And then we say murishani. So the murishani is M-U-L-I-S-H-I. Murishani.
Murishani. So that is to say, how are you? Murishani. And the person answering will say
gwino. Ndi fye gwino. I am fine. Ndi fye gwino. I am fine. So we look at that word,
gwino. Again in D, we are going to use N. N-D-I-N-D-F-Y-E. Okay.
Ndi fye gwino. So you see, the vowels are coming in I am fine. I am fine. Ndi fye gwino. I am fine.
Okay. So today we are just simply looking at how the vowels work. Okay. The E-R-A-E-O-U.
A-E-E-O-U. So these letters are very important in pronouncing Bemba words.
In pronouncing Bemba words.
So we are going to another
phrase. If you say, what is your name?
Ni we nani? Ni we nani ishina? Ishina. Okay. This is what is your name? This is ni we nani ishina? What is your name?
Then you say, ishina ria ndi ni ne. Okay. My name is Ishina. Ishina.
Ishina ria ndi
ni ne. Then you put your name. My name is, then you pronounce your name.
So that is how the Bemba language go. But as for today, I think we just have to concentrate on knowing the R-A-E-O-U.
Then with these simple phrases, ni we nani ishina? What is your name? Ishina ria ndi ni ne.

I think this today forms our lesson. We just go through this, repeat over and over. And once we get this, the alphabet, then the consonants, the words we use, the other letters are what we call the consonants, combining with the vowels. That's how the Bemba language is spoken, or is formed, or is written.

I think for today, we'll end here, and then we'll continue with another lesson, lesson two. We keep on building. I think in lesson two, we'll dwell mainly on how letters are formed, how consonants and the vowels make the Bemba words. Thank you very much.

Mr. Kaputu Oswald, a native language teacher based in Zambia. I'm able to teach how to write, speak, and translate. Subscribe to my channel today and learn how to speak, write, and translate one of the most languages used in Zambia, Bemba.
